sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on the center of the face changed 2 cells in the border region. So clicking anywhere on the face (not just on the eyes) shifts the borders. This confirms that clicking on the face shifts the borders.
Let me check the current border state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current borders
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current borders:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'B']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) in [0, 63]:
            print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Also check eyes
print("\nCurrent eyes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"  Eye id={n['id']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

The face has two eyes that can move. The borders can be adjusted. What's the natural goal state?
One possibility is that the goal is to make the eyes "look" at the border transition point. For example, if the border transition is at column 32 (equal borders), the eyes could be looking at column 32.
Another possibility is that the goal is to have the eyes at a specific position relative to the face outline. For example, the eyes could be at the top of the face or at the center of the face.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to make the eyes "disappear" by moving them to a specific position? For example, the eyes could disappear when they're at the top of the face or when they're at the border transition point.
